name: git-commit-helper
description: Generate descriptive commit messages by analyzing git diffs. Use when
capabilities: Analyze git staged changes to understand what was modified
Generate commit messages following conventional commits format
Apply appropriate commit types (feat, fix, docs, refactor, etc.)
Include breaking changes notation when detected
Add issue references and footer tokens to commits
Review commit message quality against best practices
author: Joseph OBrien
securitySummary: Documentation-only skill containing pure markdown files. No executable code, network calls, file system access, or external commands. The static analyzer misidentified markdown code blocks as Ruby/shell execution. All 90 findings are false positives.
valueStatement: Writing clear commit messages takes time and consistency. This skill provides structured guidance, conventional commit formats, and templates to generate descriptive commit messages automatically based on staged changes.
userTitle: Generate commit messages from git diffs
limitations: Does not execute git commands directly
Does not modify or commit changes to repositories
Does not integrate with GitHub, GitLab, or other remotes
Does not access git history beyond what user provides

useCases.title: Write clear commit messages
useCases.description: Generate consistent, descriptive commit messages following industry standards for personal projects.

useCases.title: Maintain commit history
useCases.description: Create standardized commit messages across team repositories for better changelog generation.

useCases.title: Document changes properly
useCases.description: Write professional commit messages that help contributors understand changes and automate releases.

promptTemplates.title: Basic commit help
promptTemplates.prompt: Help me write a commit message for my staged changes. Here is the git diff output:

promptTemplates.title: Conventional format
promptTemplates.prompt: Write a conventional commit message for these staged changes using feat, fix, or other appropriate types:

promptTemplates.title: Breaking changes
promptTemplates.prompt: Generate a commit message that indicates a breaking change in the API for these staged changes:

promptTemplates.title: Multi-file commits
promptTemplates.prompt: Write a comprehensive commit message for these multiple related file changes, grouping them logically:

bestPractices: Use imperative mood for descriptions (add, not added; fix, not fixed)
Keep the subject line under 50-72 characters
Explain WHY the change was made, not just WHAT changed
antiPatterns: Using vague messages like update, fix stuff, or changes
Including implementation details in the summary line
Using past tense (fixed, added instead of fix, add)

faq.question: What commit types are supported?
faq.answer: feat, fix, docs, style, refactor, test, chore, perf, build, ci, and revert. Each maps to specific semantic versioning impacts.

faq.question: What is the maximum length for commit messages?
faq.answer: Subject lines should be under 72 characters. Body paragraphs can be longer but stay concise.

faq.question: Does this integrate with Git hosting platforms?
faq.answer: No. This skill generates message text. You apply the message when running git commit manually.

faq.question: Is my repository data safe?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill only processes git diff text you provide. No repository data is stored or transmitted.

faq.question: Why are my commit messages not working?
faq.answer: Ensure you provide the full git diff --staged output. The skill needs to see what actually changed to generate accurate messages.

faq.question: How is this different from commit templates?
faq.answer: This skill actively analyzes your changes and generates appropriate messages. Commit templates only provide formats you fill manually.
